coredata.dc:title: An antifouling electrochemical aptasensor based on poly (glutamic acid) and peptide for the sensitive detection of adenosine triphosphate
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Alan
Hong, Xiaocheng
Zhang, Yue
Yin, Jifang
Sheng, Liying
Li, Zhenjiang
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive and antifouling electrochemical aptasensor based on L-Glutamic acid (L-Glu) and peptides was constructed for the detection of adenosine triphosphate (ATP). Firstly, poly L-Glu (p-L-Glu) as the substrate material was directly grown on glass carbon electrode (GCE) via facile and controllable electrodeposition method. Subsequently, the ATP aptamer and peptides were immobilized on the modified electrode, respectively. The prepared aptasensor exhibited a wide linear range of 0.01 pM-1.0 μM, a low LOD of 0.01 pM. The aptasensor also had excellent selectivity, reproducibility and stability. Moreover, due to the synergistic effect of the p-L-Glu and peptides, the aptasensor had the same linear range towards ATP in 10% serum solutions as in ultrapure water, indicating its potential for clinical analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: L-Glutamic acid
Peptide
Adenosine triphosphate
Aptasensor
Antifouling ability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the aptasensor fabrication procedure
Antifouling ability measurements of the aptasensor
The investigation on selectivity, reproducibility, reusability and stability of ATP aptasensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work reported here was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. 51672144, 51572137, 51702181, 52072196, 52002199, 52002200, Major Basic Research Program of Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province under Grant No. ZR2020ZD09, Shandong Provincial Key Research and Development Program (SPKR&DP) under Grant No. 2019GGX102055, the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province under Grant No. ZR2019BEM042, the Innovation and Technology Program of Shandong Province under Grant No. 2020KJA004, Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (Grant No. 2019A1515110933), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2020M683450) and the Taishan Scholars Program of Shandong Province under No. ts201511034. We express our grateful thanks to them for their financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication of the aptasensor.
(A) XPS spectra of p-L-Glu, (B) SEM image of p-L-Glu, (C) TEM image of p-L-Glu.

(A) DPV curves obtained at different modified electrodes in PBS (0.2 M, pH = 6.5) containing 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and 0.1 M KCl, (B) XPS spectra of (I) p-L-Glu, (II) p-L-Glu/aptATP and (III) p-L-Glu/aptATP/Peptide.

The nonspecific adsorption measurements of GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP (A) and GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP/Peptide (B) in HSA solution, the nonspecific adsorption measurements of GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP (C) and GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP/Peptide (D) in human serum solutions with different concentrations.

(A) DPV response of the aptasensor incubated in ATP solution with different concentrations. (B) Corresponding response plot of the aptasensor. Inset showed the corresponding calibration plot (R2 = 0.998) (The ATP samples were prepared with ultrapure water).

(A) DPV response of the aptasensor incubated in ATP solution with different concentrations. (B) Corresponding response plot of the aptasensor. Inset showed the corresponding calibration plot (R2 = 0.991) (The ATP samples were prepared with 10% human serum solution).
Comparison of various biosensors for the detection of ATP.

head.ce:title: An antifouling electrochemical aptasensor based on poly (glutamic acid) and peptide for the sensitive detection of adenosine triphosphate

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive and antifouling electrochemical aptasensor based on L-Glutamic acid (L-Glu) and peptides was constructed for the detection of adenosine triphosphate (ATP). Firstly, poly L-Glu (p-L-Glu) as the substrate material was directly grown on glass carbon electrode (GCE) via facile and controllable electrodeposition method. Subsequently, the ATP aptamer and peptides were immobilized on the modified electrode, respectively. The prepared aptasensor exhibited a wide linear range of 0.01 pM-1.0 μM, a low LOD of 0.01 pM. The aptasensor also had excellent selectivity, reproducibility and stability. Moreover, due to the synergistic effect of the p-L-Glu and peptides, the aptasensor had the same linear range towards ATP in 10% serum solutions as in ultrapure water, indicating its potential for clinical analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Accurate, rapid and specific detection of disease biomarkers directly in complex biological media is of extremely importance for early clinical diagnosis [1,2]. Over the past few decades, a great deal of attention has been paid to the exploration of new technologies to detect early signs of various diseases, including colorimetry [3], fluorescense methods [4,5], surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy [6], electrochemical technique [7,8] and so on. Among these, electrochemical technique provides an excellent and popular choice for disease biomarkers detection due to its advantages such as high sensitivity, user-friendly operation, simple preparation, and low cost [9,10]. However, enormous challenge still exists when performed in complex biological media (e.g., human plasma, blood serum, or urine) due to severe nonspecific adsorption or fouling of various proteins on the electrode interfaces, which may make the biosensors poor sensitivity or even lose selection performance. Therefore, it is urgent to design antifouling electrochemical biosensors for detecting targets in real biological media.

At present, to overcome this issue, some antifouling materials such as zwitterionic polymers [11,12], oligo (ethylene glycol) (OEG) [13,14], poly (ethylene glycol) (PEG) [15,16] and peptides [17,18] have been exploited for the fabrication of an antifouling biosensors. Among these antifouling materials, due to the advantages of easy synthesis and low cost, the peptides have been used widely to construct antifouling biosensors. The sequence of the peptides can be rationally designed to obtain the peptides with charge neutrality, which can reduce the adsorption of charged proteins on the electrode surface [19]. In addition, the deprotonated carboxyl groups (–COO−) and protonated amine groups (–NH3 +) on the peptides can easily form hydrophilic interactions to improve the hydrophilicity of the interface, which also can reduce the protein adsorption [20]. Nowadays, many antifouling biosensors based on peptides have been reported. For instance, Ye and co-workers synthesized a peptide with the sequence of CRERERE and demonstrated that its antifouling properties to single protein adsorption [21]. Cui et al. reported a peptide (EESKSESKSGGGGC) that exhibited strong antifouling feature to human serum albumin [22]. Although these peptides exhibited excellent antifouling performances, their applications in the construction of biosensors and assaying in complex media have rarely been reported.

For the fabrication of the antifouling surfaces, it is important to immobilize them onto substrate materials with outstanding conductivity, stability and biocompatibility. Amino acids have been widely used as the substrate material to modify the electrodes via electrodeposition method because of its good biocompatibility and stability, excellent conductivity, as well as environmentally friendly [23]. Moreover, amino acids contain abundant amino groups, carboxyl groups and a variety of oxygen-containing functional groups, which can provide active sites for immobilizing the aptamers and proteins [24–27]. As a kind of amino acid, L-Glutamic acid (L-Glu) not only has excellent characteristics of the amino acids, but also can be modified by electrodeposition method on the surface of the electrode as a matrix for immobilizing biomolecules. Therefore, based on the above analysis, it can be inferred that the L-Glu with prolific carboxyl groups can be used as the substrate material to construct antifouling aptasensor with the good detection performance. To our knowledge, the relevant report has hardly been found.

Inspired by the status above, herein, an antifouling and sensitive aptasensor towards ATP was constructed (as can be seen in Scheme 1 ), of which, poly L-Glutamic acid (p-L-Glu) as the substrate materials was electrodeposited on glass carbon electrode (GCE). Then, the ATP aptamer (aptATP) was immobilized on GCE/p-L-Glu to bind with the target. Lastly, the peptides (EKEKEKEPPPC) were anchored on GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP to block unbound sites on the electrode surface and improve the antifouling ability of the aptasensor. The aptasensor constructed in this study exhibited a wide linear range, a low limit of detection (LOD) and high sensitivity. In addition, the aptasensor also have excellent selectivity, reproducibility and stability. Based on the synergistic effect of the p-L-Glu and peptides, the aptasensor had the same linear range towards ATP in 10% serum solutions and the ultrapure water, indicating its potential for clinical analysis.

L-Glutamic acid was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). ATP, cytosine triphosphate (CTP), uridine triphosphate (UTP) and guanosine triphosphate (GTP) were obtained from the Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl-aminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were supplied by Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Human serum albumin (HSA), immunoglobulin G (Ig G) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Aladdin Reagents (Shanghai, China). The aptamer of ATP (aptATP, 5′-NH2-ACC TGG GGG AGT ATT GCG GAG GAA GGT-3′) was purchased from SBS Genetech Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). The peptides (EKEKEKEPPPC, purity > 95%) were ordered from Bank Peptide Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Hefei, China). Human serum was provided by Huangdao Chinese Medicine Hospital (Qingdao, China). All other reagents used in this paper were analytical reagents grade without any purification.

The peptide solution (0.4 mg mL−1), HSA solution (1.0 mg mL−1) and human serum solutions with different concentrations were all prepared with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (0.2 M, pH = 7.4). Moreover, the solutions used in the study were all prepared with ultrapure water. Furthermore, PBS (0.2 M, pH = 7.4) was used to rinse the electrode after each step.

All electrochemical measurements were implemented on CHI 660E electrochemical work station (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd., China). In the measurements, the bare and modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, d = 3.0 mm) worked as the working electrode, a platinum wire worked as the counter electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) worked as the reference electrode. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250, U.S.A.) was used to analyze the chemical composition of the samples. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SM-6460LV, Japan) was used to characterize the morphology of the substrate materials.

Firstly, GCE was pretreated according to our previous work [28]. Secondly, p-L-Glu was electrodeposited on GCE via cyclic voltammetry (CVs) scanning from −0.8 V to 2.0 V and p-L-Glu modified electrode was noted as GCE/p-L-Glu. Thirdly, 0.4 M EDC and 0.1 M NHS solution were used to activate the carboxyl on GCE/p-L-Glu for 1 h, and then the activated electrode was immediately immersed into 1.0 μM aptATP solution for 2 h at 4 ℃. For the above process, the carboxyl on L-Glu will covalently bind with amino on aptATP, which can achieve the effectively attachment of the aptamers and the modified electrode was defined as GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP. Finally, after GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP was activated again by using 0.4 M EDC and 0.1 M NHS solution for 1 h, it was immersed into 0.4 mg mL−1 peptide solution for 2 h and the modified electrode was noted as GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP/Peptide. After each step, the modified electrode was rinsed by PBS (0.2 M, pH = 7.4).

Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was used to monitor the current response in each measurement. It was performed from −0.4 V to 0.4 V with a amplitude of 0.05 V in 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- PBS solution (pH = 6.5) containing 0.1 M KCl. All electrochemical measurements were implemented at room temperature.

XPS analyses were carried out to analyze the surface composition of the p-L-Glu grown on electrode and the result is shown in Fig. 1 A. It can be seen from Fig. 1A that carbon (C1s), oxygen (O1s) and nitrogen (N1s) were characteristic elements of the p-L-Glu and appeared in XPS spectra, demonstrating that the p-L-Glu was deposited on the electrode successfully. The morphology of the p-L-Glu directly grown on the electrode was characterized by SEM. As shown in Fig. 1B, the p-L-Glu electrodeposited on the electrode exhibited a lamellar structure, which can provide more active sites for the fixation of ATP aptamer. The above results demonstrated that p-L-Glu with thin sheet structure was successfully electrodeposited on the electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the aptasensor fabrication procedure
ce:section.ce:para: DPV was employed to characterize the fabrication procedure of the aptasensor. The current responses of bare GCE, GCE/p-L-Glu, GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP and GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP/Peptide were shown in Fig. 2 A. Apparently, when p-L-Glu was electrodeposited on GCE, the current response of GCE/p-L-Glu was much higher than that of bare GCE, which was mainly ascribed to the excellent conductivity of p-L-Glu. Moreover, p-L-Glu electrode material had no negative charge, so electron transfer between the GCE/p-L-Glu surface and the active solution cannot be hindered. The above analysis confirmed that p-L-Glu was successfully electrodeposited on GCE. After the aptATP was anchored on the electrode via amide bond, the current response of GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP was significantly reduced. This may be attributed to the electrostatic repulsion between the phosphate group on the aptamer and the active probe, indicating the successful attachment of aptATP on the modified-electrode. Subsequently, the current response was further reduced when the peptides were attached on the electrode (GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP/Peptide). This is mainly because the peptides itself were non-conductive, and when they were fixed to the electrode surface, the electron transport between the electrode surface and active solution was hindered, thereby reducing the current response. The aforementioned results also proved the successful immobilization of the peptides. According to the current response changes of the different electrodes, it can be initially demonstrated that ATP aptasensor was successfully constructed.

In order to further prove the successful construction of ATP aptasensor, XPS was used to characterize the elemental changes of electrode materials during the modification procedure, as can be seen in Fig. 2B. When p-L-Glu was electrodeposited on the electrode (spectra I), the peaks of carbon (C1s, 285 eV), oxygen (O1s, 532 eV) and nitrogen (N1s, 400 eV) can be significantly observed, which were mainly from p-L-Glu. The result indicated that the p-L-Glu was successfully electrodeposited. When aptATP was immobilized on the modified-electrode (spectra II), the peak of phosphorus (P2p) located at 135 eV can be observed, indicating the successful immobilization of aptATP. After the peptides were introduced to improve the antifouling ability, a characteristic peak of sulfur (S2p, 164 eV) can be observed from the spectra III, which is mainly derived from the thiol group (-SH) of the peptides. Based on the above analysis, it was found that p-L-Glu, aptATP and peptides were successfully modified on the surface of the electrode, and it was further proved that the ATP aptasensor was successfully constructed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifouling ability measurements of the aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The nonspecific adsorption of the proteins on the aptasensor surface can seriously impair its performance. Therefore, improving the antifouling ability of the aptasensor is of great significance for accelerating its application in clinical analysis. In this paper, HSA (single protein) and human serum solution (complex protein) were selected as the contaminating materials to investigate the antifouling ability of the aptasensor. The results are shown in Fig. 3 . It mainly shows the current response of GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP (Fig. 3A) and GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP/Peptide (Fig. 3B) before and after immersed in 1.0 mg mL−1 HSA solution, respectively. It can be seen that the current response of GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP was significantly decreased after immersion in the HSA solution. The results indicated that HSA was strongly adsorbed on the electrode surface, thereby reducing the current response. But for GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP/Peptide, it had no significant change in current response after immersion in HSA solution. The aforementioned results indicated that GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP/Peptide had excellent antifouling ability, which was mainly attributed to the good hydrophilicity of the p-L-Glu and peptides.

For an ideal aptasensor, further improving its antifouling performance in complex protein solutions is the key to enhance the practical application ability. Therefore, the human serum solution was used as a complex protein to test the antifouling performance of the aptasensor. The results are shown in Fig. 3C-D. It can be seen from Fig. 3C that as the concentration of human serum solution increased, the current response of GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP decreased significantly. When the serum concentration reached about 60%, the current response only remained 59% of its initial value. The possible reason is that human serum solutions contained a variety of proteins and inorganic salt ions, which can be adsorbed on the electrode surface and reduce the contact area between the electrode and the active solution, thereby reducing the current response. In Fig. 3D, when the peptide-modified electrode (GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP/Peptide) was immersed in human serum solution with different concentrations, the current responses were reduced with very small amplitude. When GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP/Peptide was immersed in 60% serum solution, the current response of the electrode was 154.7 μA, still remaining 93% of its initial value. More importantly, when GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP/Peptide was immersed in a pure serum (100%) solution, the current response was only reduced by about 33%. The results indicated that only less protein had been adsorbed on the surface of GCE/p-L-Glu/aptATP/Peptide, which was mainly attributed to the excellent antifouling ability of the p-L-Glu and peptides. Based on the above analysis, the aptasensor had the excellent antifouling ability in both single and complex protein solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The investigation on selectivity, reproducibility, reusability and stability of ATP aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: In the actual sample detection, other interfering substances may affect the combination of the aptamer and the target. Therefore, selectivity is an essential requirement for an ideal aptasensor. In this study, 1.0 μM CTP, 1.0 μM GTP, 1.0 μM UTP, 1.0 μM BSA and 1.0 μM IgG were chosen as the interfering substances to evaluate the selectivity of the aptasensor. Fig. S1 shows the current response difference (ΔI) of the aptasensor towards the interfering substances. In Fig. S1, for CTP, GTP, UTP, BSA and IgG, the ΔI of the aptasensor are all lower than 10 μA, indicating that the interfering substances were not substantially bonded to the aptasensor surface. But for ATP, the ΔI of the aptasensor is about 45 μA. Even in the presence of all substances (ATP, CTP, GTP, UTP, BSA and IgG), the sensor's ΔI still remain around 42 μA, indicating the excellent selectivity of this aptasensor.

Reproducibility is also an important indicator for evaluating the performance of an aptasensor. Therefore, the reproducibility of the as-prepared aptasensor was explored. Firstly, five aptasensors were prepared in parallel under the optimal conditions. Then, the aptasensors were used to detect 1.0 μM ATP sample, and the current response of each aptasensor was shown in Fig. S2. As can be seen from Fig. S2, the current response of the five aptasensors is 122.8 μA, 123.4 μA, 124.7 μA, 120.9 μA, and 124.2 μA, with an average of 123.2 μA, and a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.20%. Based on the above analysis, the aptasensor had satisfactory reproducibility.

Besides the reproducibility, the reusability is another important parameter for practical application. And it is explored by measuring the current response five times and the results are shown in Fig. S3. Apparently, the current response of the as-prepared aptasensor did not show significant signal loss up to four repeated measurements and the RSD is 1.98%, suggesting the as-prepared aptasensor presented excellent reusability.

In order to evaluate the stability of the aptasensor, five aptasensors prepared in parallel were immersed in PBS (0.2 M, pH = 7.4) solution for 7 days at 4℃, and then the aptasensors were used to detect 1.0 μM ATP sample. The current response of the aptasensor towards 1.0 μM ATP is shown in Fig. S3. As can be seen from Fig. S3, the current response of the five aptasensors to ATP samples is 120.9 μA, 118.6 μA, 121.3 μA, 119.7 μA, and 117.2 μA, respectively, with the RSD of 1.41%, which is not much different from the current response of the prepared aptasensor. The results indicated that the aptamer on the aptasensor surface still had good activity to bind with ATP, which also proved the excellent stability of the aptasensor.

In order to evaluate the practical application capacity of aptasensor, the aptasensor was used to detect ATP sample in 10% human serum solution. The results are shown in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5A shows the current response of the aptasensor to ATP samples with the different concentrations, and Fig. 5B shows the corresponding calibration plot of the aptasensor. As can be seen from Fig. 5, for ATP samples in 10% serum solution, the aptasensor still have good linear detection performance in the range of 0.01 pM-1.0 μM (R2 = 0.991), the linear equation is ΔI = 5.41261LogC + 78.1241. Comparing the results in Fig. 4 and Fig. 5, the aptasensor had consistent detection performance for the ATP samples solved in ultrapure water and in 10% human serum solutions. The results were mainly due to antifouling ability of the p-L-Glu and peptides, which prevented the adsorption of proteins and inorganic salts ions in the serum on the electrode surface, thereby increasing the practical application capacity of the aptasensor. In the actual analysis, although the concentration of the serum is very high, it can be achieved for detecting the target by diluting the samples. Therefore, the aptasensor prepared in this study had practical application potential.

In summary, a sensitive and antifouling aptasensor was constructed for detecting ATP via electrochemical method. Poly L-Glu as the substrate material was electrodeposited on GCE, and it not only improved the conductivity of the electrode, but also provided the matrix for the immobilization of ATP aptamer. The aptasensor prepared in this study possessed a wide linear range of 0.01 pM-1.0 μM, a low LOD of 0.01 pM and a high sensitivity of 582.9 μA·μM−1·cm−2. The aptasensor also had good selectivity, reproducibility and stability. Moreover, based on the synergistic effect of p-L-Glu and the peptides, the ATP aptasensor can effectively detect ATP in 10% serum solutions, which have the same detection performance towards ATP as the testing result in ultrapure water, indicating its enormous application potential in clinical analysis.

Alan Meng: Methodology, Conceptualization. Xiaocheng Hong: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Yue Zhang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jifang Yin: Data curation. Liying Sheng: Writing - review & editing. Zhenjiang Li: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work reported here was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. 51672144, 51572137, 51702181, 52072196, 52002199, 52002200, Major Basic Research Program of Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province under Grant No. ZR2020ZD09, Shandong Provincial Key Research and Development Program (SPKR&DP) under Grant No. 2019GGX102055, the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province under Grant No. ZR2019BEM042, the Innovation and Technology Program of Shandong Province under Grant No. 2020KJA004, Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (Grant No. 2019A1515110933), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2020M683450) and the Taishan Scholars Program of Shandong Province under No. ts201511034. We express our grateful thanks to them for their financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106365.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antifouling Strategies for Selective In Vitro and In Vivo Sensing
Chem. Rev.

Biosens. Bioelectron.

Bioelectron. 79 (2016) 205-212.
